title: Retail: Automated Stores & Shelves
concept: Computer vision in retail automates checkout, tracks shelf inventory, and analyzes customer traffic patterns.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is Apple's native framework for mobile deployment?
exercises.explain: Apple uses CoreML as its native machine learning format.

cards.title: A store without cashiers
cards.body: Imagine walking into a grocery store, picking up a carton of milk, and simply walking out. No lines, no scans, no cashiers. Computer vision makes this possible by tracking which items you pick up and charging your account automatically!

cards.title: Computer Vision in Retail
cards.body: In retail, vision systems perform shelf monitoring (detecting out-of-stock items), automated checkout (using object detection to identify items in cart), and traffic heatmaps (tracking passenger flow through the store).

cards.title: An automated store detective
cards.body: Think of retail vision like a virtual security guard. Instead of watching to catch thieves, they watch to help you check out, listing items you put in your bag, and reminding the store owner when the peanut butter shelf is empty.

cards.title: Just Walk Out Technology
cards.body: Smart stores use hundreds of cameras mounted on the ceiling. Using object tracking and pose estimation, the system detects when a customer removes a specific cereal box from a shelf and adds it to their virtual receipt.

cards.title: Camera occlusion is a massive challenge
cards.body: You might think tracking items is easy because products have bright labels. In reality, customers block cameras (occlusion), put items back in the wrong place, or hold items close to their bodies, making detection highly complex.

cards.title: Retail Vision Tasks
cards.body: Common vision applications in a modern store:

left.text:
Detects products in carts
Uses multi-class detectors
Eliminates barcode scanning
High accuracy requirement

right.text:
Detects empty spots on shelves
Classifies product types
Triggers restock alerts
Handles camera occlusion

exercises.prompt: Which vision task helps a retail store manager identify when a product is completely out of stock on shelves?
exercises.explain: Shelf monitoring systems detect gaps in shelf displays to trigger automated restocking alerts.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Occlusion (when an object is blocked from view by a hand or body) is a major challenge for automated checkout cameras.
exercises.explain: True. If a customer blocks the camera with their hand, the system cannot see what product was taken.

exercises.sentence: A map showing where customers spend the most time walking or standing in a store is called a traffic ___.
exercises.explain: Traffic heatmaps plot movement density to help store managers optimize layouts.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a typical challenge for product classification in a grocery store?

options.text: Slight variations between packaging sizes (e.g., 12oz vs. 16oz soda)
The lack of high-resolution digital cameras

exercises.explain: Packaging items can look identical in shape and color, requiring fine-grained classification to distinguish between different volumes or flavors.

exercises.prompt: You are building a checkout-free checkout system. A customer picks up a can of soup, blocks the camera with their sleeve, and drops it into their bag. What strategy helps resolve this occlusion problem?

options.text: Use multiple camera angles and fuse data from shelf weight sensors.
Increase the model's learning rate during training.
Make the customer look directly into a camera and scan the soup manually.
Use a larger convolutional kernel on the main camera.

exercises.explain: In real-world retail systems, fusing multiple camera feeds (multi-view) and using weight sensors on the shelves (sensor fusion) resolves occlusion errors.
